content.text: Resolution is not the end of the customer relationship — it is the beginning of the trust-repair phase. Enterprise customers in particular judge AI platform reliability not only by whether incidents happen, but by how you communicate, follow through on commitments, and demonstrate learning. A well-executed post-incident follow-up process often converts a frustrated customer into a loyal one. A poor one — missing the post-incident report deadline, not following through on promised actions — can cost a renewal.

attrs.alt: Post-incident customer follow-up timeline showing five checkpoints: resolution, T+24h CSM check-in, T+5 business days PIR published, T+30 days commitment review, next billing cycle SLA credit

content.text: Published PIR on status page + enterprise email
Update email confirming preventive actions complete
Credit applied automatically; CSM confirms receipt
The resolution email (covered in the templates topic) is the first checkpoint. For the follow-up process, the important addition is logging the closure properly:
Mark the status page incident as 'Resolved' with final component statuses all Operational
Archive (do not delete) the incident Slack channel — it is the permanent record for the post-mortem
Close the PagerDuty incident and annotate it with the resolution summary
Create follow-up tasks in Jira: PIR due date, CSM call scheduled, commitment tracking

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

@dataclass
class IncidentClosure:
    incident_id: str
    resolved_at: datetime
    severity: str
    duration_minutes: int
    affected_customer_ids: list[str]
    sla_breached_customer_ids: list[str]
    ic_name: str

def close_incident(closure: IncidentClosure, jira, statuspage, slack) -> None:
    """Create all follow-up tasks on incident resolution."""
    biz_days_5 = _add_business_days(closure.resolved_at, 5)
    days_30 = closure.resolved_at + timedelta(days=30)

    # Post-incident report task
    jira.create_task(
        title=f"PIR: {closure.incident_id}",
        assignee=closure.ic_name,
        due=biz_days_5,
        labels=["post-incident-report", closure.severity],
    )

    # CSM check-in tasks for each enterprise customer
    for customer_id in closure.affected_customer_ids:
        customer = get_customer(customer_id)
        if customer["tier"] == "enterprise":
            jira.create_task(
                title=f"CSM check-in: {customer['name']} after {closure.incident_id}",
                assignee=customer["csm"],
                due=closure.resolved_at + timedelta(hours=24),
                labels=["csm-followup", closure.incident_id],
            )

    # SLA credit tasks
    for customer_id in closure.sla_breached_customer_ids:
        customer = get_customer(customer_id)
        jira.create_task(
            title=f"SLA credit: {customer['name']} for {closure.incident_id}",
            assignee="finance-team",
            labels=["sla-credit", closure.incident_id],
        )

    # Archive Slack channel
    slack.archive_channel(closure.incident_id)
Enterprise customers expect a call within 24 hours after a SEV1 that affected them. This is not a post-mortem — that comes later with the PIR. This call is a
Who Should Be on the Call
Primary relationship owner; leads the call
Can answer technical questions about what happened
Present for SEV1; optional for SEV2
Validates impact from their side; may have data on their downstream effects
For major customers or extended outages, a senior presence signals seriousness
— CSM opens, thanks customer for time, sets agenda
— IC or tech lead gives a plain-language summary of what happened, no jargon
— Open floor for customer to describe their downstream impact; listen without defending
— Walk through the PIR timeline and the specific preventive actions you are committing to
— Address any remaining questions; take unclear ones as action items with dates

Opening: "We wanted to reach out directly because we know yesterday's incident affected your production workloads. Before we share what we know so far, we'd like to hear from you — what impact did you see, and is there anything urgent we can address right now?"

On root cause: "What we found was a configuration drift that caused memory limits on our inference pods to fall below actual requirements. This caused pod restarts under normal load. We've corrected the configuration and added automated drift detection to our CI pipeline."

On trust: "We understand this affects your confidence in our reliability. We are publishing a full post-incident report by {date} that will include the complete timeline, root cause analysis, and the specific engineering changes we are making."
What Not to Say on the Check-In Call
Do not minimise: 'The outage was only 39 minutes' — the customer knows how long it was
Do not over-promise: 'This will never happen again' — no one can guarantee that
Do not deflect: 'Our provider had issues' — you own the customer relationship regardless of cause
Do not bring the legal team to a relationship call unless the customer asks for it
Do not open the call by reading the timeline — ask about their impact first

The Post-Incident Report (PIR) is the definitive external account of the incident. It must be accurate, written in plain language, and specific about what went wrong and what you are doing to prevent recurrence. A vague PIR ('we are improving our monitoring') is worse than a specific one ('we are adding memory limit drift detection to our CI pipeline with an SLA of Q3 completion').

# Post-Incident Report
**Incident ID:** INC-2024-03-15-042
**Service:** Inference API
**Severity:** SEV1
**Duration:** 39 minutes (14:18 – 14:57 UTC, 2024-03-15)

## Executive Summary
A configuration drift caused inference pods to exhaust available memory under normal load,
resulting in elevated latency and errors for approximately 40% of requests in the us-east-1a
region. The issue was mitigated by rerouting traffic while the configuration was corrected.

## Timeline
| Time (UTC) | Event |
| --- | --- |
| 14:18 | Monitoring detected P99 latency exceeding 5 seconds |
| 14:20 | Engineering team declared SEV1 |
| 14:35 | Root cause identified: memory limit configuration drift |
| 14:40 | Traffic rerouted away from affected zone |
| 14:45 | Latency began recovering |
| 14:57 | Full service restored; incident resolved |

## Root Cause
A Helm values file was updated during a routine infrastructure change without updating
the corresponding memory limit for inference pods. The mismatch was not caught by our
automated configuration validation because the validation only checked for syntax errors,
not semantic correctness against runtime memory profiles.

## Impact
- **Duration:** 39 minutes
- **Affected requests:** approximately 40% of Inference API requests in us-east-1a
- **Affected users:** enterprise customers and self-serve users with workloads in us-east-1a

## What We Are Doing
| Action | Owner | Target Date | Status |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Add memory profile validation to CI pipeline | Platform team | 2024-03-29 | In progress |
| Implement automated memory limit drift alerts | Observability team | 2024-04-05 | Planned |
| Regional traffic rerouting automation | Infrastructure team | 2024-04-12 | Planned |

## How We Detected It
Automated monitoring detected the latency spike within 2 minutes of onset. The alert threshold (P99 > 5s for 5 minutes) meant there was a 5-minute delay between
first degradation and alert fire. We are reviewing the alert threshold to reduce detection latency.
Sending the PIR to Enterprise Customers

Subject: Post-Incident Report — {Your Company} Inference API — {date}

Dear {customer_name} team,

As promised, we are sharing the full post-incident report for the Inference API
disruption on {date}.

You can read the full report here:
https://status.yourcompany.com/incidents/{incident-id}

Key points:
  Root cause: {one sentence, plain language}
  Duration: {N} minutes
  Your estimated impact: {description}

Commitments:
  We have committed to {N} specific engineering changes to prevent recurrence.
  We will update you on completion of these changes by {30-day check-in date}.

If you have questions about the report or want to discuss the technical details,
please reply to this email or reach out to {csm_name}.

— {Your Company} Engineering Team

Enterprise customers who read your PIR will note your commitments. If you promised three engineering changes and have not mentioned them again in 30 days, they will assume you forgot. The 30-day review email closes this loop:

Subject: Follow-Up — {Your Company} Inference API Incident — Corrective Actions Update

Dear {customer_name} team,

Following up on the Inference API incident on {original_date}, we wanted to update
you on the preventive actions we committed to in our post-incident report.

Completed actions:
  [x] Memory profile validation added to CI pipeline — completed {date}
  [x] Memory limit drift alerts deployed — completed {date}

In progress:
  [ ] Regional traffic rerouting automation — expected completion {date}

We will send a final update when the remaining action is complete.

If you have seen any related issues since the incident or have questions,
please do not hesitate to reach out.

— {csm_name}, Customer Success
  {Your Company}
Track these metrics across all incidents to evaluate follow-up process health:
PIRs published within 5 business days / total SEV1+SEV2
Actions completed by target date / total committed
Check-ins completed within 24h / total enterprise SEV1s
Send a brief survey to enterprise customers 7 days after resolution. Keep it to three questions — longer surveys get ignored:

Subject: Quick question about your experience during last week's incident

Hi {first_name},

We'd love 2 minutes of your time to understand how we did during the
{date} Inference API incident.

1. How satisfied were you with our communication during the incident? (1-5)
   1 = very dissatisfied  |  5 = very satisfied

2. Did our post-incident report answer your questions? (Yes / No / Haven't read it)

3. Is there anything specific we could have done better? (free text, optional)

Your feedback directly influences how we improve our incident response process.

— {csm_name}, {Your Company} Customer Success
If a customer experienced the same type of incident twice within 90 days, the standard follow-up process is insufficient. Use a
VP of Engineering or CTO sends a personal email within 48 hours, acknowledging the pattern explicitly
Offer a 1-hour executive briefing with the customer's technical leadership (not just the CSM call)
Publish a reliability commitment document — a written set of specific, measurable changes with dates, not generic promises
Schedule a 60-day follow-up (not just 30) to confirm all commitments are complete
Consider proactive SLA credits even if the SLA was not technically breached — goodwill matters
Ignoring the repeated-incident pattern and sending the same standard follow-up is a reliable way to lose an enterprise contract at renewal.
Customer feels forgotten; may escalate to legal or churn
PIR task created at resolution with automatic deadline reminder
Customer cannot assess whether the problem is fixed
Specific actions with owners, dates, and measurable outcomes
Check-in is mandatory for all enterprise SEV1s regardless of duration
30-day review reveals nothing was done; customer finds out at next incident
Jira tasks for every commitment created at PIR publish time
Customer has to ask for what they are contractually owed
Finance automation applies credit in next invoice cycle without customer action
Heavy users feel de-prioritised; churn risk
Automated email at T+5 days with PIR link for self-serve users who had errors during the window
Post-incident follow-up has five checkpoints: resolution confirmation, CSM check-in (T+24h), PIR (T+5 business days), commitment review (T+30 days), SLA credit (next billing cycle)
The CSM check-in call is a relationship conversation, not a technical post-mortem — ask about the customer's impact before explaining yours
PIRs must be specific: named actions, owners, target dates — not generic 'we will improve monitoring' language
Track every PIR commitment in Jira and send a 30-day update confirming completion
SLA credits should be applied automatically by Finance; customers should not have to request what they are owed
Repeated incidents within 90 days require executive escalation, not the standard follow-up process
Measure follow-up quality with PIR on-time rate, commitment completion rate, CSM check-in completion, and post-incident CSAT
